items.q: Does Hooke's law apply to a spring for any amount of stretching?
items.correct: No — it only holds up to the limit of proportionality; beyond that the relationship between force and extension is no longer linear.
items.misconception: Yes — Hooke's law applies to any spring at any extension.
items.random_wrong: Only when the force is small enough to be ignored.
items.why: Students apply F = kx universally without noting the limit of proportionality, leading to over-prediction at large extensions.

items.q: What happens to a metal wire stretched beyond its elastic limit?
items.correct: It deforms plastically and will not return to its original length when the load is removed.
items.misconception: It snaps immediately at the elastic limit.
items.random_wrong: It becomes more elastic and can stretch further.
items.why: Students treat the elastic limit as the breaking point, conflating it with the ultimate tensile strength or fracture point.

items.q: What is the difference between heat and temperature?
items.correct: Heat is the energy transferred due to temperature difference; temperature measures average kinetic energy of particles.
items.misconception: Heat and temperature are the same thing — both measure how hot something is.
items.random_wrong: Heat is measured in degrees Celsius and temperature in Joules.
items.why: Everyday language uses 'heat' and 'temperature' interchangeably ('turn up the heat'), so students don't distinguish them physically.

items.q: Heisenberg's uncertainty principle was published in:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1927. “1913” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Maximum binding energy per nucleon occurs around mass number:
items.why: Correct answer: 56. “12” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Year Bohr published his hydrogen atom model?
items.why: Exact answer: 1913 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
